data.title: Expedia Group: Price Recovery In Line With Valuation And Strategic Business Model
data.description:
If you remember, I reiterated my bullish, or buy, rating on Expedia Group, Inc. (EXPE) with some caution regarding its valuation in my previous coverage. Not long after, the selloff took place until it hit $188. I understand the situation is in line with market volatility, fueled by macroeconomic woes amid the Middle East tension. Even so, the price drop opened new buying opportunities as the stock bounced back to its current price. At this point, valuation still makes sense, while technicals show new entry points.

Expedia Group: Growth Didn't Go On Vacation

Macroeconomic volatility may have been intense in recent months due to stubborn inflation, tariff wars, and geopolitical tensions. But travel and tourism remained unperturbed in line with favorable travel perceptions and changing work setups. The efforts of the central banks, particularly the Fed, were not futile, as this may have helped boost economic activity, productivity, and wages. Tourism benefited as well, and big players like Expedia Group, Inc. reflected this in their most recent performance.

Its operating revenue amounted to $3.5B, up by 11.4% YoY from $3.1B. This YoY growth was much higher than in my previous coverage at only 8.6%. This suggests EXPE’s sustained growth and improved performance. This can also tell us that while revenge travel has already peaked, the hype has continued. As you can see, its gross bookings rose to $27.0B versus $24.4B. More interestingly, all quarters in 2025 had higher gross bookings, showing stronger travel demand than in 2024.

We can attribute it to slower inflation and the policy easing cycle that encouraged spending. The prevalence of remote and hybrid work setups also offered flexibility to millions of employees and employers. This allowed them to work while traveling. In 2025, 51% of employees were on hybrid setups, while 28% were fully remote. So, it’s not surprising that travel demand and hotel/flight bookings remained resilient. This also helped EXPE maintain strong pricing power. As you can see, EXPE reported higher booked room nights, booked air tickets, and ADR. Another driver was its increased presence in international markets like Europe and Asia Pacific.

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses rise in line with its continued expansion and technological innovation. However, their combined amount only increased by 5.3%, so revenue growth easily offset it. This shows that its increasing capacity and improved capabilities became more fruitful and efficient. As a result, its operating margin became a double-digit figure at 11.8% versus 6.7% YoY.

Potential Risks

As a business involved in travel and tourism, EXPE faces several risk factors to note.

One is stubborn global inflation. In the US, for instance, it remains above the 2.0% target. This suggests that the Fed is still having a hard time keeping it under control. Also, positive inflation means prices are still increasing faster than the desired level. Tariff wars may exacerbate it due to their tendency to raise domestic prices across various countries.

But a more important aspect to consider today is the intense situation in the Middle East. Despite the ceasefire deal, oil prices are still skyrocketing close to their three-year highs. This can also make fuel prices higher, so traveling may become more expensive. On top of that, this affects multiple industries like logistics and shipping. And because they are crucial in the supply chain, their spillovers will extend to other industries. In the end, inflation may speed up. The story does not end here; purchasing power and travel budgets among individuals and households may decrease. The budget of companies for advertising may also weaken. If these two things happen at the same time, EXPE may generate lower advertising fees and booking commissions. Lastly, it may incur increased expenses due to higher prices. It may have to increase wages so its employees can keep up with inflation.

Risk Mitigants

Despite these potential challenges, I believe that EXPE has some strengths to protect its business.

One of the things I like about it is its business model. It may not be unique, but it's strategic. Its partnership with Wells Fargo and Mastercard allows it to offer OneKey credit cards. This offers it an extra revenue stream. Wells Fargo is one of the largest banks in the world, while Mastercard is a large global payments network in credit cards. So, it won't be surprising to know about its extensive market coverage and huge customer base. By integrating OneKey into partner platforms and marketing it, EXPE receives payments. Despite market risks, I believe that credit card demand and usage will remain resilient. Why? It will most likely support discretionary spending on travel. Also, central banks like the Fed have implemented a policy easing cycle in H2 2025, which could have raised credit cardholders. With that, EXPE may benefit from resilient travel demand. Additionally, this credit card partnership helps EXPE manage its customer acquisition costs because customers and credit cardholders are typically loyal. They have these credit cards used for specific purposes, say travel. As a result, EXPE will not have to spend a lot on ads like Google Travel Ads to acquire and/or re-acquire customers. In the end, it earns revenues in two ways: booking commissions and a portion of credit card fees/payments.

Another core strength of EXPE is its B2B growth. It remains its key revenue growth driver. After all, B2B allows it to earn revenues in various ways, even in single transactions. If travelers book a hotel or flight through EXPE's ads, EXPE earns both booking commissions and ads. And if they use their credit cards issued by EXPE's partners, they also earn through it. This is wise because this appears to be a high-income and low-cost model.

Its technology stack is also a potential driver. Its One Key platform connects Expedia, Hotels.com, and Vrbo and makes them a unified traveler experience. With that, travelers can have a single login for these platforms. Also, this means shared rewards. If they get rewards or points from booking a flight on Expedia, they can spend the rewards or apply the discount to their booking on Vrbo. That will be beneficial on the demand side. On the business side, this allows EXPE to improve the speed of its AI deployment for improved and more streamlined marketing. I think this is another aspect that continues to help EXPE manage its costs and expenses despite its sustained innovation and expansion.

More importantly, EXPE maintains its robust fundamentals, which assure us of its liquidity and longevity amid macroeconomic volatility. Its cash and cash equivalents remain massive at $5.4B. This can cover all maturing debt within the year as well as its accounts payable, deferred revenues, and taxes payable. Additionally, the maturity profile of its borrowings is decent. Over 70% of the total will not mature within a year. Also, cash and cash equivalents can cover 88% of the total in a single payment. So, it’s not surprising that its Net Debt/EBITDA remains low at 0.3x, which means that EXPE only needs 0.3 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. This also shows that it has low leverage. Hence, it still has a large buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

2026 Views

Less than two weeks before the release of Q1 2026 results, the company expects positive trends. And I think that still makes sense despite the escalation of the Middle East tension. After all, EXPE's main business is still in North America despite its continued expansion in Europe and APAC.

Aside from resilient travel demand, I think EXPE could have also still capitalized on volatile situations. For instance, travelers may have taken advantage of the interest rate cuts to avail of credit cards and book their travel plans. They may have also decided to travel while the full-blast impact of the tension and oil price hikes has not fully materialized yet. After all, spring already started last month.

To have a glimpse of its performance, I decided to make an estimation of its core operations based on its own revenue guidance. To be more precise, I made high and low estimates. Meanwhile, I estimated the cost of revenue (10%) and SG&A expenses (69%) by setting them as a percentage of revenue based on the recent quarterly trends. But to be more conservative, I also added 2% to include the potential impact of inflation. So, cost of sales and SG&A expenses will be 12% and 71%. Note that I excluded other operating items from the computation, including impairment, legal reserves, and restructuring. These items are typically exceptional, so they are not part of the actual course of business or its day-to-day operations.

In the end, EXPE will still be profitable since revenues remain large enough to cover all costs and expenses. All EBITDA will be higher than in Q1 2025 at only $355M ($2.988B - $357M - $1.757B - $199M - $320M).

Low Estimates High Estimates

Low Estimates With

Inflation Escalation

High Estimates With

Inflation Escalation
Revenue $3.32B $3.37B $3.32B $3.37B
Cost of Revenue $332B $337B $398B $404B
SG&A Expenses $2.29B $2.33B $2.36B $2.39B
EBITDA $698M $708M $565M $574M

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model, or DDM. The annualized dividend is now $1.92 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.48. In turn, the dividend growth rate increased to 12.44%. However, I increased my risk premium to 6.5% to be more conservative versus 5.5% in my previous coverage and the market standard of 5.0%. That way, I can price in more risks due to the ongoing Middle East tension that has sent oil prices above the 2025 highs. Lastly, Beta increased to 1.33, which means that the stock has become less volatile than in my previous coverage. Nonetheless, its price swings are still larger than the S&P 500. With that, my new TP, or target price, is $289.41. This shows that EXPE is still reasonably cheap today.

I also used the P/E Ratio to assess it. Currently, it trades at 25.62x its EPS versus the average of 29.08x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $285.23, which is close to my DDM TP. Now, an investor may say that it is not exactly cheap relative to previous years. And if not for the 2022 P/E, which appears as an outlier, EXPE won’t really be underpriced. However, it is also important to note that its earnings growth in those years has been impressive as well. In fact, the CAGR was 34.42%. The PEG is 0.89 (25.62/28.54), which means that EXPE is only trading at 89% of its earnings growth. And if you believe that the trading multiple should equate to its earnings growth, the TP will be $281.73. This proves that the stock still has some upside and that the selloff/pullback was overdone.

Technicals

EXPE’s technicals show attempts to become bullish once again as the price rebound continues. The price is trending above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing that the current momentum is stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is increasing again, which means that recent market sentiments have been quite optimistic once more. Also, we should note that the price has been increasing with higher highs and lows since its rebound, showing a high possibility that the uptrend may hold.

Its MACD and RSI explain the recent selloff. This is the crucial aspect I failed to notice in my previous coverage. Before the selloff, the price high increased from $274 on November 12 to $302 on January 9. However, the highs of the two indicators on those dates decreased, which meant that fewer buyers entered when the price became expensive. This was a confirmed bearish divergence since both indicators showed the same trend. Coincidentally, the stock became overbought, leading to profit-taking and a correction. But now, the MACD line is increasing again above the signal line within positive territory along with the histogram. This implies that buying volume is still increasing and outpacing selling volume. The selling volume in the past two days is also in line with RSI because the stock became overbought again. But now, buying room has expanded again, while the two indicators remain consistent with the price trend. They prove that the bullish trend holds today.

Conclusion

Expedia Group, Inc. remains robust with its continued evolution and expansion to cope with the market trends amid macroeconomic volatility. This is supported by its strategic partnership and solid liquidity. Its valuation also justifies its uptrend and shows some upside potential. Technicals now show renewed bullish signals as rebound attempts from the selloff continue. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Caterpillar: The 43x P/E Data Center Trap
data.description:
Investment thesis

Caterpillar (CAT) is a Strong Sell at $808, trading at a tech-like 43x P/E that ignores its lack of operational leverage and a $350 mean-reversion fair value. The last 12 months stock rally is fueled by the hyperscale data center narrative. The headline backlog of $51 billion masks a business where costs are outpacing revenue growth even when volumes are booming. Management is eroding shareholder value by funding $5 billion in buybacks, executed at a historic 95th percentile of CAT's 10-year valuation, consuming the majority of their $7.45 billion in free cash flow.

The Margin pressure

Caterpillar's $51 billion backlog record looks like global demand, but looking at the segments shows the margins are deteriorating.

Construction industry sales increased by 15% in the fourth quarter to $6.9 billion... Fourth quarter profit Construction Industries decreased by 12% versus the prior year to $1.0 billion. The segment's margin was 14.9%, a decrease of 470 basis points versus the prior year. The margin decrease was primarily due to higher manufacturing costs driven by tariffs, which had an impact of about 600 basis points on margins

- CFO Andrew Bonfield, Q4 2025 Earnings Call

All three primary segments posted double-digit sales growth. Yet only Power & Energy converted that volume into profit growth (+25%), while the others saw concerning decreasing profit, year over year.

Margin compression during revenue growth

Like mentioned, a 43x P/E reminds you of a tech company, which implies structural operating leverage. Caterpillar does not prove operational leverage over the last 5 years.

As shown above, you can see how the net margin is largely unchanged despite healthy revenue growth over the last 5 years. Revenue climbed from $64.81B to $67.59B between 2024 and 2025. Operating margins fell 370 basis points over the same period. The margin profile is compressing while the top line expands.

Wall Street consensus estimates out to FY2029 seem to agree, as they show how little structural leverage exists in the sector.

Analysts forecast a $22 billion revenue growth across this 4-year window. The net margin only moves from 13.1% to 20.1%.

In a software company, $22B in incremental revenue would flow almost entirely to the bottom line.

At Caterpillar, with a 30% margin, 70 cents of every new dollar gets consumed by the cost of goods sold. There is no margin expansion story here.

However, tariffs have a rising effect on CAT. CFO Andrew Bonfield disclosed on the Q4 2025 earnings call that net incremental tariff costs reached $1.5-1.8B for full-year 2025 after mitigation efforts. For FY26, Bonfield guided incremental tariff costs of $2.6B, with $800M in Q1 alone.

Capital allocation at peak multiples

Capital allocation 101 dictates only buying back shares if the current share price is below the true intrinsic fair value of the stock. Buying above fair value is destroying shareholder value.

Although lowering the rate since the 2024 peak, management continues buying back more than $5 billion worth of shares during 2025 at peak multiples.

To add to the damage, in 2025, shareholder returns exceeded free cash flow by $510 million. Management generated $11.74B in operating cash flow, allocated $4.29 billion to CapEx, and still returned $7.96 billion to shareholders.

The math does not balance without drawing down cash or issuing debt. FCF of $7.45 billion covered dividends ($2.75 billion) but not the full $7.96 billion returned to shareholders through buybacks and dividends. CAT is distributing cash above its means at the top of its valuation cycle.

The dividend yield has fallen to a 5-year low of 0.75%, attributed to the increasing share price, substantially lower than the 5-year average of 1.75%.

The dividend yield has fallen below its 5-year average while the FCF yield tracks lower in tandem. The spread between FCF yield and dividend yield remains positive, confirming the payout is safe, disregarding the buyback cash drain. If management continues to favor recent buyback rates, the dividend could be cut, frozen, or subjected to sticky low dividend growth in the time to come.

Valuation: 95th percentile of its own history

Plotting the daily TTM P/E ratio over the last decade further visualizes the extreme valuation.

The histogram plots every trading day's trailing P/E over the last decade. The cluster between 10x and 20x is where Caterpillar spent most of its trading history. The red dashed line at 43.0x sits in the extreme right tail, above 94.8% of all observed values.

Two brief spikes during earnings collapses in 2017 and 2020 produced comparable or higher multiples, but those were driven by temporarily suppressed EPS rather than increasing share price. The current boom in growing EPS is showing the market is pricing CAT as a tech stock.

Back to proper valuation

Two separate mean-reversion models result in the same grounding of the share price.

Dividend yield theory: TTM DPS of $5.89 divided by the 5-year average yield of 1.75% = $337 fair value. At $808.87, that implies 58.3% downside.

P/E mean reversion: TTM EPS of $18.81 multiplied by the 5-year average P/E of 19.4x = $365 fair value. That implies a 54.8% downside.

Blended: a simple average of the two = $351, leaving a rounded fair value anchor at $350.

The forward earnings picture does not resolve the valuation problem.

Assume Caterpillar hits every growth target through FY29 with zero execution errors. The stock still trades at 21.1x three years from now. For grounding, that is assuming no economic downturn, no new unforeseen events with negative impact until the end of the decade. Even if EPS doubles from current levels, the implied forward P/E does not drop below 20x, leaving no margin of safety at $808.

Anchoring on competitors

Compared to competitors, Oshkosh trades at 14.8x. PACCAR, despite its own strong cycle, sits at 27.8x (only!) with a 2.16% yield. Deere, which carries its own AI-adjacent precision agriculture narrative, trades at 32.7x. Caterpillar at 43.0x with a 0.75% yield is the most expensive name in heavy machinery by a wide margin, with its multiple 31% higher than its closest expensive peer.

Risks: where I could be wrong

I'm assuming the data center build-out is cyclical, at least to some extent after the initial build-out, and that multiple compression is inevitable. I could be wrong on both counts.

The AI build-out is permanent. If global grid demands for AI power persist for a decade, CAT's P&E backlog never shrinks, and all backlogs convert. Their EPS base builds permanently, and the 43x multiple becomes the new normal for the stock - highly unlikely.

Looking at street estimates, however, I must admit I'm more bearish than the lot. 26 analysts currently cover Caterpillar with a consensus recommendation of Buy. Their 12-month price targets range from $430 (low) to $920 (high), with a median of $781.

This article's blended fair value of $350 sits below the most bearish 12-month target on Wall Street. The distinction matters: analyst targets represent where the stock may trade over the next 12 months given current momentum and earnings trajectory. The $350 fair value is a mean-reversion equilibrium price, where the multiple would land if it normalized back to 5-year historical averages. Those are different questions. If momentum keeps up, the share price might stay elevated for an extended time.

Conclusion

Despite top line growth, Caterpillar is struggling to convert to cash. The cash they are generating is spent on buybacks at peak multiples and keeping the dividend alive. The valuation is not grounded in realities. At a 43x P/E multiple, CAT needs to deliver tech leverage to justify the current valuation.

Caterpillar reports Q1 2026 on April 30th. Consensus expects $4.64 EPS on $16.42B in revenue. The quarterly print does not change this thesis. Beat by 10%, and the stock still trades above 40x. Miss by 10%, and the multiple expands further into the red zone. A cyclical manufacturer at the 95th percentile of its own historical P/E distribution is expensive on its own merits.

Rating: Strong Sell; Fair Value: $350

data.title: MercadoLibre Could Deliver 25% Annual Earnings Growth And 3-4x Returns By 2031
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe it's likely that MercadoLibre (MELI) will compound earnings at over 25% annually over the next 5 years as the business's Fintech segment continues to scale and margins expand modestly. In the event that such actually happens, I think the stock can have a plausible route of providing 3-4x returns over the next decade.

Mercado Pago is a driving force behind this. The market still largely frames MercadoLibre as an e-commerce business, but fintech now accounts for roughly half of the company's revenue, is growing faster than commerce, and carries structurally higher monetization potential.

More importantly, Mercado Pago sits on top of MELI's marketplace, which will help build a closed-loop system where commerce drives payments, payments generate data, and data improves underwriting and credit expansion. That loop increases engagement, raises spend per user, and strengthens the entire business's long-term economics.

Meanwhile, I also think that MELI's current margins understate the company's long-term earnings power, since management is still reinvesting in logistics, credit, and user growth. Should even a portion of that expenditure stabilize with the maturity of the ecosystem, I believe it would be highly likely that margins across the business would grow.

Combined, I believe $MELI has a credible path to 25% annual earnings growth over the next 5+ years driven by sustained 20% annual revenue growth, modest margin expansion, and a growing mix of higher-quality fintech earnings. With MELI currently trading a bit below $1,900 per share at 37x forward earnings, the stock doesn't seem terribly expensive if the business delivers earnings growth in line with analysts' consensus estimates.

MercadoLibre Business Overview

MercadoLibre operates two core segments: Commerce and Fintech. The Commerce division encompasses the marketplace, logistics network, and merchant tools that combine to achieve transaction volume throughout the platform. That scale helped to build MercadoLibre's initial Latin American advantage.

Mercado Pago is one of the most important pieces of the business today. What began as a payments solution to the market has now expanded to become more of a financial platform, incorporating digital wallets, merchant acquiring, and credit. Fintech is now more than 40% of total revenue and is expanding more rapidly than commerce, becoming a far bigger driver of the long-term earnings power of the company.

The size of this ecosystem is already significant. In Q4 2025, MercadoLibre announced 120 million unique buyers and 78 million fintech monthly active users, underscoring that Mercado Pago itself is an independent financial platform integrated into a larger network. Also, in Q4 2025, commerce revenue increased 40% compared to the previous year, while fintech revenue increased 51%. Management still views fintech as one of its most significant structural opportunities.

What makes this model powerful is the interaction between the two segments. Commerce drives payment volume, payments generate proprietary data, and that data improves underwriting and credit expansion. Meanwhile, credit and financial services increase user engagement and monetization, which reinforces the entire system. As a result, MercadoLibre is building a closed-loop financial layer on top of its commerce network that has the potential to drive higher margins and faster earnings growth over time.

Fintech Is The Real Growth Engine

The core of the thesis is that fintech is already scaling into a meaningful, higher-quality earnings driver for the business.

In Q4 2025, Mercado Pago reached nearly 78 million monthly active users, growing about 27% year over year. At the same time, assets under management rose to $18.8 billion, up roughly 78%, while the credit portfolio expanded to $12.5 billion, increasing nearly 90% from the previous year.

This shows how MercadoLibre is becoming a full financial platform with deposits, lending, and investment products, each of which carries higher monetization potential than transactions alone.

More importantly, this growth is happening inside MercadoLibre's ecosystem. Unlike standalone fintechs, Mercado Pago has direct access to merchant activity, transaction data, and user behavior across the marketplace. That allows the company to underwrite credit with context, which can lead to better risk-adjusted returns as the loan book scales.

If MercadoLibre can continue growing its credit portfolio at a high rate without a meaningful deterioration in credit quality, fintech has the potential to become the primary driver of earnings growth over time. That is what makes this segment so important to the broader 25%+ annual earnings growth thesis.

Margins Are Temporarily Depressed Due to Reinvestment

I believe MercadoLibre's margins today understate the company's true earning power. Normalizing margins can therefore serve as an eventual lever to drive earnings growth.

Operating margins have declined from peak levels near 18% to roughly 8-10% more recently, reflecting a period of heavy reinvestment. Management has made it clear that initiatives like lower free-shipping thresholds, logistics expansion, credit growth, and other reinvestment activities have reduced operating margins by around 500-600 percentage points. With the business seeing 10.1% operating margins in Q4 of 2025, this suggests the company could theoretically lift operating profit by 50-60% by ceasing reinvestment activities.

Gross margin has also been impacted because some of the deliberate shipping subsidies and logistics investments flow through to the cost of goods sold. This is why gross and operating margins have fallen in tandem.

There is also clear evidence that this reinvestment is being used to drive growth. Cross-border trade revenue grew 74% in Q4 2025, and advertising revenue grew 67%, both of which are high-margin segments that will help increase profits in the future. And fintech is growing rapidly, providing another source of higher-margin revenue.

Why MELI Could Compound At 20%+ Annually

The base case revenue, margin, and EPS estimates in the valuation model below reflect analysts' consensus estimates. The base case assumes MercadoLibre revenue grows at a 22.1% CAGR to 2031, which is a slowdown from its historical growth rate but still a very healthy rate of growth given it is driven by ongoing expansion of its fintech platform and ecosystem.

However, analysts' consensus estimates project that net income margins will grow from current levels of 6-7% to 11.5% by 2031. This is quite plausible to model, and could be achieved by simply adding the current 500-600 basis points of margin sacrificed to reinvestment to net income over the next 5 years.

For the sake of simplicity, let's assume that the stock trades at the same forward earnings multiple (37x) as it does today.

22.1% annual revenue growth and a little bit of margin improvement yield around 33% annual EPS growth. At these rates, the valuation model supports a 36.5% annual return, or 4.3x upside by 2030.

However, there are a couple of key assumptions used in this model to keep in mind. This model uses 5-year estimates, and marginal changes to the company's growth rate or operating margin can have a material effect on the stock's long-term value.

Additionally, the model assumes the valuation multiple will remain at the current 37x forward P/E. If we assume the stock's P/E will drop to 25x by 2030, then the 5-year IRR would fall to 25.7% compared to 36.5%. That's still a very good annual return, but it illustrates how multiple compressions can quickly deteriorate returns.

MELI has historically traded slightly above peers. Today, the stock sits below its historical average:

What Would Break The Thesis

The investment case rests on the company's Fintech segment scaling profitably and margins recovering toward historical levels. On the fintech side, if underwriting quality deteriorates, rising delinquencies would erode the segment's earnings contribution. Competition or increased funding costs could also put pressure on take rates, in which case user growth and increasing AUM wouldn't be as important.

The second key assumption is that the current margin squeeze is the result of reinvestment and, as such, is only partly reversible. However, if the margin compression is permanent, due to the business needing to invest heavily to compete, then the value of future earnings is lower than this thesis suggests. This possibility is further complicated by increasing competition in Brazil and Mexico, where MercadoLibre may need to continue to heavily subsidize sellers, build out their logistics capabilities, and respond with increased marketing spend to acquire customers.

Even if the business executes well, if investors put a lower multiple on MELI because they expect slower growth ahead, then this could cancel out much of the price appreciation. Latin America also carries its own macroeconomic risk, and currency devaluations, inflation, or economic slowdowns across Brazil, Mexico, or Argentina could negatively impact how the business performs.

Conclusion

MercadoLibre's investment case hinges on becoming Latin America's leading commerce and financial infrastructure platform. Fintech already drives a growing share of revenue, and that mix shift should support higher monetization over time.

Company management is actively holding margins down via investment, with Q4 investments driving down operating margins by 500-600 basis points. If they boost user engagement and retention, it's likely the margins will expand over time.

Assuming MercadoLibre continues to grow revenues strongly, expands fintech profitably, and can grow margins by even modest amounts, the company can grow earnings by 25%+ annually. At the stock's current valuation, this should lead to attractive long-term returns.

data.title: Akzo Nobel: Quality Company At Discount Price For 2026
data.description:
Paints, coatings, and similar chemicals are part of a very fundamental driver of our industrial world. Almost nothing in the industry can be manufactured and sold either to a commercial or consumer customer without receiving some sort of paint and coating. The coating, whichever form it takes, serves as the primary protective barrier against various types of environmental degradation, which in turn not only increases the aesthetic value of the item but also its lifespan.

The use of coatings and paints increases value, increases operational liability, reduces maintenance, and provides safety in harsh conditions. It's fair to say that leading companies in this sector serve a primary function and field, which is why it's a sector that I am interested in.

I have covered Akzo Nobel (AKZOY) previously and maintain a position in the company. I am now ready to increase that position even further. The reason I'm ready to do this is that the market continues to undervalue Akzo Nobel - and also because some of the market trends may have been more long-term than I saw.

Long-term market trends or headwinds do not frighten me. I, in fact, believe them to be a counter-indicator when they go below a certain point. Over 90% of my returns are made investing in companies that, by other investors, are viewed as unfavorable for one reason or another.

My job here, my job for you, is to sift the companies that deserve to be trading lower in the long term from the companies where the headwinds are only a signal of misappreciation or misunderstanding on the part of the market.

The market not only always misappreciates, but it also overreacts - both to the positive and the negative. My job is to see, as said above, when this overreaction is wrong.

I believe the reaction to be deeply negative here, and in this article, I will justify the reason for thinking so. You can find my previous piece on the company here.

Akzo Nobel - Upside From Paints is Significant, Even More So After a 10% Drop

I last covered this company about 7 months ago. The development since then, relative to my rating, has not been flattering. The latest results we have are only a few days old at the time of updating this article, but the results were not flattering. The company saw a decline in the top line, with a sales decline of about 1%. The company also saw significant impacts from its divestments in the Indian market, despite increasing product prices. Gross margins were up about 1% (due to pricing), EBITDA margins were up (again, pricing), and adjusted EBITDA was up 7% at comparable scope and FX. We need to be careful with the adjustments here, of course, because on a non-impacted basis, things were down.

What is important to understand about Akzo Nobel, and the reason I believe the market is severely underestimating the company relative to more conservatively exposed first-market paints and coatings companies, is Akzo's emerging market coverage and exposure. The declines in share price due to what the company reports as revenue weakness imply to me that there is an underappreciation of significant company margin expansion. Then there's the potential of Axalta, which I'll get into further below. It's a merger that the company is doing.

What the market is also, in my view, underestimating is the context in which the company delivers what amounts to stable revenues (almost) and growing margins. The market is one of the worst for paints and coatings in many years. Despite this significant pressure, Akzo Nobel is successfully driving margin expansion. Structural cost savings are certainly part of it - and these are obviously not recurring - but they are not the only part of it.

Akzo Nobel's FCF is also clearly improving - if you look at the filings, you can see indicators like net cash outflows declining much more in the last few years. This implies that the company is managing its working capital much, much better than it did before.

The company's exposure to China and the currently weakening Chinese market is a problem. But it's also one that's being overinterpreted a little bit. The company's volume trends were strong elsewhere, and the fact that the stock is being punished for poor headline growth (China), while other markets are growing well above the industry and market average, at about 4%, tells me that the broader market is severely underestimating the company's growth potential.

In short, I believe that the market analytics are overfocusing on headline decline and struggles with price/mix while declining or neglecting to focus on bottom-line improvements, the quality of the earnings rather than the earnings itself. It's a question of quality over quantity, as it so often in fact is. And, of course, there's obviously the Iran crisis as well, which is impacting things.

Then there's Axalta - the upcoming merger. Despite only light improvements expected for 2026, the company expects flat volumes and pricing to offset, at best, logistics and feedstock while maintaining sub-€360M CapEx and a leverage of about 2x at a stable dividend, but the merger is what has the potential for non-organic upside.

The current closing estimate is 2026-2027. Votes, though I expect this to go through, are coming in July of this year. The merger with Axalta is a merger of companies of equal size because Axalta is, in fact, a significant paints and coatings company in its own right. But the merger is a good idea because its portfolios are highly complementary from an asset, production, and market perspective. It will put Akzo in a position to not only compete with PPG and Sherwin-Williams but to "beat" the company at its own game.

Akzo's main strength lies in decorative paints and marine and protective coatings, including powder. Meanwhile, Axalta is a leader in refinishing. The two merging means customers will have less time to wait; it will create a full-spectrum and close to vertically integrated offering of about 100 brands in total that covers the entire value chain. The competition cannot really match this all that well.

Preliminary numbers for things like savings and synergies are obviously to be taken with a great degree of salt. What's been put forward is about $600M in savings within the first 3-4 years. These efficiencies will come through things like procurement, footprint optimization, and SCM.

This merger brings with it, obviously, potential for significant margin optimization. Initial numbers suggest bringing EBITDA margin to about 20%, because Axalta, while more volatile, also has higher margins.

An estimated generation of about $1.5B in FCF per year is expected. This would make the company even more of a cash printer, able to compete with the other industry leaders not only on sales but also with over 173 factories worldwide and 91 R&D facilities spending more than $400M per year on research, which will enable the company to stay ahead of the game.

One thing I find more dubious is the merger addressing slow growth - not sure it can do that - but I believe the company growing in size will increase its negotiating power, which offsets raw material/feedstock volatility somewhat, which is definitely something that is needed.

1Q26 was as good as can be expected - the company's upside is twofold. First off, the reversal in valuation will occur once end-market trends improve. Give it 1-3 years. Secondly, the upside from the merger - which I believe will be significant, despite the normal integration and merger risks.

Valuation as follows.

Valuation for Akzo Nobel - Upside is Very Much There at This Price After the Drop

The price drop in Akzo Nobel is, from one perspective, a bit weird. The company has traded at massive premiums for years, despite severe underlying volatility. Now that projections are showing stabilization in earnings, what we instead see is that the company is losing its premiumization.

This is not something that particularly scares or concerns me, because I did not buy the company expensively. I bought it cheaply.

Akzo Nobel is BBB rated, with a yield that's now close to 3.8% at a price of €52.3/share for the native. Even using a substantially discounted 15x P/E, the company generates upwards of 14.3% annualized RoR on the basis of today's estimates.

And I want to make it clear I believe the company is worth more than this - as does the market, long-term. Thus, following this logic, the price target needs to be higher than the current €52.3/share.

In my last article, I gave €72/share. Using the company's 5-year average premium, this PT is understandable, because I consider a P/E of above 20x, which, by the way, is the peer-relevant P/E as well, to be valid. It would imply today, at my fair value target, something like a 30-35% annualized RoR inclusive of dividends, which would make the company one of the best basic materials companies to invest in from a combined perspective of safety and upside. Take into consideration extremely limited leverage here as an upside as well, deserving a bit of a premium.

However, I'm willing to concede that I might have been a bit optimistic about that premium 6-7 months ago. Using a more conservative growth estimate - cutting growth from 8-9%, even though I consider this realistic, to about 4-5%—offers the advantage of a better, conservative forecast. Using, therefore, 17-19x P/E, we find ourselves at a PT of not €72/share, but about €66/share.

What this means is the following.

Using a €66/share estimate and forecasting 4-5% growth with a 19x P/E offers a 15% annualized upside or above. At €52.3/share, the native price today, the upside is actually closer to 23-25% per year, depending on your estimate.

More importantly, I see no scenario where a €45-€55/share PT would be justified for the longer term. The company's ability to generate cash, its safety, and its margins are simply too good for this.

So I reiterate "BUY", I put the company at €66/share PT for the native AKZA ticker, and I will probably be adding some Monday morning.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk for the company would still be, in my view, the forward potential for growth, savings, and the correlation to slowdowns. The risk of worse performance than expected is there once the savings and value extraction from its current review are done. Bearish arguments can also be made that Akzo Nobel may have already extracted the lion's share of improvements from what's possible here, which means that further efficiencies will be harder to "get" - though I would now argue against this on the basis of Axalta. As mentioned, the performance coatings segment is also extremely competitive, and this is worth considering as well (because coatings tend to have lower margins than standard paints). Again, though, Axalta will serve to bring better returns here.

For the time being I believe valuation and short- to medium-term upside imply this to be an attractive investment. I cut my PT to €667/share but maintain "BUY".

Thesis

Akzo Nobel is a leading paint and coatings supplier with an IG rating and the most attractive dividend yield in its entire peer group, especially compared to Sherwin-Williams. Because of this and valuation trends, I view the company as very attractively positioned, and while the company's future growth is more likely to come from inorganic sources than organic ones, I can see a potential upside even at a conservative valuation.

I would still, as of April 2025, put a fair value of 18-19x P/E for the company for the long term, implying a PT of around €66/share. I view the company as more interesting now, given the state of the market.

At a current share price of just south of €54/share, this makes Akzo Nobel an attractive "BUY". This price comes from the ADR AKZOY to a price target of $25.8/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of €66/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: May 2026 Monthly
data.description:
The global capital markets will enter May with a sense of transition rather than resolution. Neither the Federal Reserve nor the European Central Bank nor the Bank of Japan meets in the month ahead. The market is inclined to see the third consecutive hike by the Reserve Bank of Australia, but the other G10 central banks that meet in May, Norway's Norges Bank and Sweden's Riksbank, are most likely to stand pat.

The aggressive tightening cycle that investors had penciled in during the first part of the Middle East war has been unwound, though not completely. Markets have been forced to recognize that the inflation threat is not over. The March readings were firm, and the April reports are unlikely to offer relief. The spill over into core prices is becoming more visible, which complicates the narrative.

The geopolitical backdrop has shifted but not settled. The war in the Middle East is unresolved, and the disruptions are multifaceted and will linger. Shipping routes, insurance costs, and regional supply chains will not snap back quickly. The regional geopolitics will not return to status quo ante. Pakistan is a nuclear power, and its regional role has increased. US bases were once seen as protection and may be re-evaluated.

Federal Reserve in Flux

In Washington, the Federal Reserve faces its own uncertainty. Chair Powell’s term ends mid-May. The Justice Department ended its probe into the Federal Reserve's renovations on April 24, and this removes the main obstacle to the confirmation of Kevin Warsh to succeed Jay Powell as chair. There is no FOMC meeting in May, and we expect Warsh will lead the next meeting in mid-June, when the Summary of Economic Projections is updated. We continue to suspect that there is a reasonably good chance that Powell stays on to complete his term as governor (January 2028).

In his confirmation hearings, Warsh made it clear that his tenure was going to be a break from the period of great continuity from Bernanke through Yellen, and Powell. The targeted measure of inflation may change, and Powell's average inflation rate target may be jettisoned. The Summary of Economic Projections will be rethought. Maybe wide latitude that the regional Federal Reserve presidents enjoy in terms of publicly expressed views will be restrained.

There are two initiatives that are possible with Bessent at Treasury and Warsh at the Fed. First, there could be a new accord between the Federal Reserve and the Treasury. The markets will be sensitive to any formal erosion of the central bank’s operational independence.

Second, gold is being carried on the US government books at a little more than $42 an ounce, or around $11 bln. Some administration officials have argued that it should be revalued. Given gold’s volatility, pricing at the market seems imprudent. But some rule, like its lowest price in ten years, would allow the yellow metal to be valued at $1,000 an ounce and boost the asset on the federal government’s books to $262 bln. At $2,000 an ounce, more than a 50% haircut from market prices, it would boost the federal government's assets by more than $500 bln.

Chinese Restraint

One of the highlights of the month will be the meeting between President Trump and President Xi. The United States has acknowledged Beijing’s role in persuading Tehran to accept the ceasefire. This recognition may help create a more constructive atmosphere for the summit. China has shown more restraint than the conventional narrative in the United States tends to allow.

Despite the volatility in global markets, the People’s Bank of China has permitted a modest appreciation of the yuan. It has steadily lowered the dollar’s reference rate, around which the greenback is allowed to move two percent, although the band is rarely used even halfway. This is not the behavior of a country looking to weaponize its currency. The yuan reached its best level in three years, and the official campaign does not look over.

Beijing’s posture in the Asia Pacific region also runs counter to some of the louder commentary. With the United States drawing down parts of its arsenal and diverting assets from the region to support operations in the Middle East, some analysts expected China to test the boundaries around Taiwan. That has not happened. In fact, April saw the first visit in decades by the head of Taiwan’s opposition Kuomintang party to Beijing. Cross-strait flights will reportedly resume. These may be symbolic gestures, but symbols matter. They can signal a willingness to explore political space that had been closed off for years.

China has also cut tariffs on imports from roughly fifty African countries. The move is consistent with its long-standing strategy of cultivating influence through trade and investment rather than military presence. It also comes at a moment when the United States and China have maintained a tariff truce that extends into November. There is room for a broader deal. Taiwan and critical minerals remain sensitive issues, but both sides have incentives to avoid escalation.

The Way Forward

For investors, the challenge is to separate noise from signals. The macro narrative is not as clean as it appeared in January. Inflation is proving sticky and supply disruptions are evident across a broad range of activity. The Fed’s leadership is uncertain. The geopolitical landscape is shifting in ways that do not fit neatly into the familiar frameworks. Yet the underlying story is one of continued pressures that could presage a more acute crisis. Markets are recalibrating to a world where policy is less predictable, supply chains are more fragile, and diplomacy is more transactional.

The month ahead may not deliver clarity. What it offers instead is a chance to observe how policymakers and markets respond to a set of pressures that are unlikely to fade soon. The task for investors is to stay alert to the subtle shifts that sometimes matter more than the headline events.

Bannockburn World Currency Index

Bannockburn's World Currency Index, a GDP-weighted basket of the currencies of the dozen largest economies, recovered from its nearly 1.5% loss in March. That was its first monthly decline since last October and its largest drop since the end of 2024. It rebounded by about 1.35% through April 24. The recovery reflected the fact that all of the components of the index but the yen appreciated against the dollar. BWCI reached its best level since September 2024.

The Australian dollar fared the best with the G10 members of the index and rose by about 3.3%. It has been supported by the rate expectations and commodity exposure. Sterling was second best with around a 2% gain, followed by the Canadian dollar's 1.7% rise. The yen slipped by about 0.5%.

Among the emerging market currencies in Bannockburn's World Currency Index, the Russian ruble's almost 8% surge stands out. It fell 5.3% in March. The US has allowed more Russian oil to be bought, for which it is securing higher prices. The central bank delivered its fifth consecutive rate cut on April 24 (benchmark rate now stands at 14.5%, and additional cuts are likely). The ruble's weight in the BWCI is modest at about 2.4%. The Brazilian real, Mexican peso, and South Korean won rose 2.8%-3.2%, and together account for about 6.5% of BWCI. The Indian rupee rose by 0.6% and the Chinese yuan rose by 0.9%. Together they account for around a quarter of the index.

We are not persuaded that the new high in BWCI signals a breakout. The fog of war continues to cast a heavy pall over the investment climate and risk appetites. The US continues to bring more people and equipment into the region. The US, with its energy supplies, record exports of petroleum products, and more flexible institutional arrangements, facilitates a relatively resilient economy. The April retreat in the greenback after the March rally leaves it somewhat oversold as the month winds down. Consolidation or a dollar recovery looks likely before the medium-term downtrend resumes.

U.S. Dollar: The dollar appreciated in March, the first month of the war, but has trended lower in recent weeks. The Dollar Index matched its longest losing streak in fifteen years (eight consecutive sessions). It had rallied about 5.3% from the lows in late January that began as the US seemed to drop its threat of taking Greenland by force. By the time the war began, the Dollar Index had already appreciated 2.5%. The combination of technical factors and a fragile optimism that the Middle East war would not last long saw the dollar trend lower in April. Among the G10 countries, the US is the only one whose derivative markets are discounting the possibility of a cut. Before the war began, the futures market was discounting two cuts and around a 40% chance of a third. The war-induced swing in expectations was dramatic, and a month later, there was almost 60% chance of prices in the futures market. US Treasury Secretary Bessent's remarks about it being understandable for the Federal Reserve to monitor the supply shock seemed to be creating political space for Powell's successor to maintain steady policy without incurring the wrath of the administration. Here in late April, almost a 30% chance of a cut is discounted before the end of the year. Yet the greenback's slide has stretched the momentum indicators, and the optimism about an end to the war by the end of April is fading. This can set the stage for a counter-trend dollar bounce before what we see as its long-term decline continues.

Euro: The euro recovered what it had lost in March. It reached almost $1.1850, its best level since February 18. On an intraday basis, it met a key technical retracement of its losses since the year's high was recorded amid the furor over Greenland in late January, near $1.2080. The roughly 4.25-cent rally from the mid-March lows has stretched the momentum indicators, and the US two-year premium over Germany has stabilized after falling to its narrowest since late 2021. Before the Middle East war began, indicative pricing in the swaps market implied a better than 50% chance the ECB was going to cut rates this year. The supply shock has dashed those ideas, and at the extreme, on March 24, the swaps market had a little more than three hikes fully discounted. The pendulum has swung back a bit, but the market is pricing in two hikes this year and almost a 50% chance of a third. The first hike is not completely priced in until July, but, of course, the situation and expectations are fluid. The supply shock poses growth risks. In addition, the shortage of jet fuel and rationing in some countries will disrupt trade, businesses, and tourism. Meanwhile, the seemingly eternal struggle is once again expressing itself. Some see the crisis as an opportunity to broaden and deepen the EU bond market. Yet the opposition by some creditor countries has not relented. The lack of a joint effort risks further deepening the divergence that makes governing difficult.

(As of April 24, indicative closing prices, previous in parentheses)

Spot: $1.1722 ($1.1509) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.1712 ($1.1607) One-month forward: $1.1738 ($1.1525) One-month implied vol: 5.8% (7.8%)

Japanese Yen: The dollar reached almost JPY160.50 in March, its best level since July 2024. It traded above JPY160 in April once and barely. Instead, the greenback tested the lower end of its recent range near JPY157.50, which was also seen in March. Unlike many other pairs, the dollar-yen remained well above the level that was prevailing before the Middle East war, which was around JPY156. The war boosted uncertainty for investors and policymakers. Bank of Japan Governor Ueda had the opportunity to prepare the market for a rate hike but failed to do so. This encouraged the swaps market to unwind the expectations for a hike on April 28. The odds of a hike fell from almost 75% on April 1 to less than 7%. The probability of a hike in June is near 65%, and it is nearly fully priced in for the late July meeting. Core inflation (excludes fresh food) fell below the 2% target in February for the first time since March 2022 and remained below it in March. The economy contracted in Q3 25, and it appears to have taken two quarters to recoup the lost output. The financial crisis many economists anticipated has not materialized. The very long-end of the Japanese bond market peaked on January 20, and the 30-year yield is around 20 bp lower, and the 40-year yield is off a little more than 35 bp. The Nikkei reached a record high. Foreign investors have bought JPY7.28 trillion (~$46.5 bln) of Japanese equities in the first 16 weeks of the year compared with net sales of JPY2.07 trillion in the same year-ago period.

Spot: JPY159.38 (JPY160.31) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: JPY158.24 (JPY158.52) One-month forward: JPY158.98 (JPY159.90). One-month implied vol: 7.3% (9.9%)

British Pound: Sterling fell about 1.9% in March and recouped it fully in recent weeks. A four-month low was set at the end of March near $1.3160, and sterling rebounded to almost $1.3600 in mid-April. It had settled slightly above $1.3480 before the Middle East war began. The UK economy entered the war a bit firmer than it initially appeared. January GDP grew by 0.1%, after the initial estimate was flat, and February GDP was stronger than expected, with output rising by 0.5%. That matches the strongest monthly performance since June 2023. The March PMI, which saw the flash readings marked down in the final report, while the preliminary April readings underscore the resilience of the economy. Before the war began, the swaps market was discounting two Bank of England rate cuts fully and about a 10% chance of a third. At its peak on March 20, it was pricing in three rate hikes and almost a 40% chance of a fourth. Now, it is discounting two hikes and an almost 20% chance of a third. The UK holds local and mayoral elections on May 7. A poor showing by Labour would likely increase the political pressure on Prime Minister Starmer, who is still reeling from the criticism over the appointment of Peter Mandelson as the UK ambassador to the US, who had failed the security vetting process.

Spot: $1.3532 ($1.3259) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.3469 ($1.3306) One-month forward: $1.3535 ($1.3260) One-month implied vol: 6.7% (8.4%)

Canadian Dollar: After it reached a new high for the year at the end of March near CAD1.3965, the US dollar pulled back in the first half of April. Support was found around CAD1.3630 and the greenback recovered to CAD1.3715 area. The economy remains vulnerable, but price pressures remain too elevated to give the central bank much room to respond. Despite cutting rates 100 bp last year and 125 bp in 2024, the economy lost full-time positions in February and March, which is the most for a two-month period in nearly five years. The composite PMI has been below the 50 boom/bust level since November 2024, with one exception (October 2025). Headline inflation jumped to 2.4% in March (from 1.8%), approaching last year's high, which was the strongest pace since mid-2024. Core inflation slowed to 1.9% from 2.0%, but it is likely to rise in the coming months amid the secondary impact of higher energy costs filtering through the economy. Before the war, the swaps market had around a 40% chance of a cut discounted and its peak on March 20 was a little more than three hikes fully priced. Now, one hike and about 50% of a second are discounted. Meanwhile, as part of the review of the USMCA, the US is discussing boosting the domestic content requirement of US production, especially in the auto sector.

Spot: CAD1.3668 (CAD 1.3893) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CAD1.3683 (CAD1.3795) One-month forward: CAD1.3651 (CAD1.3875) One-month implied vol: 4.2% (5.2%)

Australian Dollar: The Australian dollar remains one of the strongest G10 currencies. It has risen in two of the three months in each of the past three quarters. In mid-April, the Australian dollar reached almost $0.7225, its best level since mid-2022. The government has cut fuel taxes and launched an interest-free loan facility for small businesses. The 26/27 budget will be unveiled on May 12. The Reserve Bank of Australia has been the most aggressive G10 central bank this year. It has hiked its cash rate twice, and hawkish rhetoric has fanned speculation of a third at the conclusion of the central bank meeting on May 5. The futures market is discounting around 75% probability. Inflation remains elevated, and household spending is robust. Still, there are some preliminary signs that economic activity is cooling. The March composite PMI plummeted to 46.6, its weakest level since November 2023. The preliminary April PMI was better, but the disruption from the Middle East war will likely take a toll. While we look for additional Australian dollar gains in the medium term, we see risk of a downside correction after likely rate hike in early May.

Spot: $0.7152 ($0.6874) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $0.7126 ($0.6950) One-month forward: $0.7149 ($0.6875) One-month implied vol: 9.0% (11.8%).

Mexican Peso: The peso rose by about 0.4% in Q1 26 and around 3.1% month-to-date in April. The peso is the fifth strongest emerging market currency this year, and Latam currencies account for three of the top five emerging market currencies. In late March, despite both headline and core inflation being above the upper end of the 2%-4% target range, the central bank cut its overnight rate target to 6.75% from 7.0%. The dollar rose to its best level of the year against the peso (almost MXN18.1650) at the end of March. The gains were unwound, and the dollar fell to MXN17.1275, its lowest level since before the Middle East War began. The low since June 2024 was recorded on February 18, near MXN17.0865. The central bank meets on May 7. While a cut is unlikely, Banxico has signaled that its concerns about economic growth could warrant another rate cut. The renegotiation of the USMCA pact poses risk for the peso. Mexico has moved to ease tensions over access to the country's electricity generation sector. We suspect there is scope for the dollar to recover into the MXN17.50-MXN17.65 area in the coming weeks.

Spot: MXN17.3787 (MXN18.1188) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: MXN17.5322 (MXN17.9743) One-month forward: MXN17.4237 (MXN18.1655) One-month implied vol: 8.9 (13.9%)

Chinese Yuan: By nearly any metric one uses, the yuan is undervalued. For reasons that have been articulated by Beijing, since around the middle of last year, it has sanctioned a steady and modest appreciation of the yuan. Since around the end of Q1 25, officials have allowed the yuan to rise. Its gain against the dollar is almost 6.5%. It has appreciated against all the regional currencies but the Malaysian ringgit. The yuan has risen by more than 12% against the yen, more than 6.7% against the South Korean won and around 1% against the Taiwanese dollar. It has also appreciated against several G10 currencies. Its trade surplus continues to cause consternation. The terms of trade (export prices to import prices) appear to be shifting. China exports manufactured goods and imports raw materials and commodities. The prices of commodities have risen relative to manufactured goods. Still, we suspect the official tolerance of yuan appreciation has not been exhausted. The easing of deflationary forces, including the first year-over-year rise in China's PPI since September 2022, and the firm 5% year-over-year Q1 GDP may encourage officials to accept more yuan gains. It may boost the chances that exchange rates are not the focus of the Xi-Trump meeting. Our next target is around CNY6.70.

Spot: CNY6.8321 (CNY6.9112) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CNY6.8407 (CNY6.9067) One-month forward: CNY6.8440 (CNY6.9145) One-month implied vol: 2.8% (3.7%)
